title: Java LinkedList
content:
### LinkedList in Java

LinkedList is a doubly-linked list implementation of the List and Deque interfaces.

---

### Creating a LinkedList

```java
import java.util.LinkedList;

LinkedList<String> list = new LinkedList<>();
LinkedList<Integer> numbers = new LinkedList<>(anotherCollection);
```

---

### LinkedList Specific Methods

| Method | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `addFirst(element)` | Insert at beginning |
| `addLast(element)` | Insert at end |
| `getFirst()` | Get first element |
| `getLast()` | Get last element |
| `removeFirst()` | Remove and return first |
| `removeLast()` | Remove and return last |
| `offer(element)` | Add as last element |
| `poll()` | Retrieve and remove head |
| `peek()` | Retrieve head without removing |

---

### ArrayList vs LinkedList

| Aspect | ArrayList | LinkedList |
|--------|-----------|------------|
| Internal | Dynamic array | Doubly-linked list |
| get(index) | O(1) | O(n) |
| add/remove at end | O(1) amortized | O(1) |
| add/remove at middle | O(n) | O(1) after traversal |
| Memory | Less overhead | More (node objects) |

---

### When to Use

- **ArrayList**: More get/set operations, access by index
- **LinkedList**: More add/remove at beginning or middle, queue/stack operations

> **Key Point:** LinkedList implements both List and Deque, so it can be used as a list, queue, or stack. It is efficient for insertions/deletions but slow for random access.
